data.title: Inseego: Sell On Aggressive Growth Projections And Elevated Headline Risk
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Inseego Corp. or "Inseego" (NASDAQ:INSG) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Two months ago, mobile broadband and fixed wireless access ("FWA") solutions provider Inseego reported Q4/2024 results ahead of expectations:

However, it is important to note that the company's fourth quarter guidance in November was well below expectations at that time.

With key carrier customers in the process of winding down product promotions and managing inventory levels, revenues in the company's core Mobile Broadband Solutions segment decreased by more than 20% on a sequential basis:

During the quarter, the company completed the sale of its Telematics business for $52.7 million in cash.

In addition, Inseego successfully restructured its outstanding, 2025 convertible notes.

In combination, these actions resulted in materially reduced net debt levels and improved liquidity:

Following the sale of the Telematics business, the company will be left with its Mobile Broadband and Fixed Wireless Access Solutions segments, as well as its high-margin "Inseego Subscribe" enterprise customer management SaaS solution for carriers.

While Inseego's SaaS offering has contributed substantially to recent service revenue increases, this is almost solely the result of the required accounting for a two-year contract renewal with a major customer that was executed twelve months ago.

Unfortunately, the company continues to experience headwinds in both its Mobile Broadband and Fixed Wireless Access Solutions segments, as outlined in more detail on the conference call:

Consistent with what we talked about on the November call, Q1 presents a slow start to 2025 on a revenue basis for three primary reasons.

First, Q3 and Q4 2024 had record revenue dynamics that were driven by unprecedented mobile hotspot execution with one of our key carrier customers, where product volumes were double and nearly triple historical norms.

Second, 2024 was the end of a special national mobile hotspot program at one of our North American carrier customers that had generated nice volumes in 2024, but that is not continuing.

And third, like we discussed around the Q4 numbers, Q1 is also impacted by lower FWA purchases from a large carrier customer that is managing their inventory levels as they transition to the next generation FWA product.

As a result, the company guided Q1/2025 well below expectations again:

Despite the weak start to 2025, management remained optimistic about the company's prospects:

While there are some anticipated revenue challenges in Q1 as we start the year, we're bullish on the prospects for the business as we move into 2025. We expect our initiatives to introduce new products and diversify the customer base to drive overall revenue growth for the full year 2025. And we expect to deliver sequential quarterly revenue growth each quarter as we move through 2025 beginning with Q2.

With management guiding for overall revenue growth for this year and consensus expectations calling for H1/2025 sales of approximately $73 million, Inseego would have to deliver at least $60 million in quarterly revenues on average for the second half which I consider aggressive, particularly after management danced around an analyst question regarding visibility into new customer and design wins on the conference call.

During the questions-and-answers session, a number of analysts appeared to be surprised by the company's ambitious second half projections and even tried to make sure that they had gotten management's statements right:

Tore Svanberg

(...) My first question is just a clarification question on the annual of the 2025 outlook. So you're saying that you would basically grow on top of the 191 that you reported for ‘24. There's no other pro forma number that we should think about in this sense.

Steven Gatoff

Hey, Tore, thanks. Yeah, that's basically right. When you're coming off the base, as Juho was just going through, there's a whole bunch of initiatives and things that we're working on now that we see an acceleration to performance in the back half of the year and exiting the year, but that should portend to growth for the total year-over-year. So that is the correct takeaway and the numbers you have are correct.

Even without the recent market disruptions caused by the Trump Administration's decision to impose harsh tariffs on key trading partners, Inseego might have struggled to live up to management's aggressive projections but given current uncertainties, I do not expect the company to come even close to current consensus expectations as key customers are likely to hold back on purchases until visibility starts to improve.

However, with the company trading at below 8x my estimated EV/Adjusted EBITDA for this year as opposed to a multiple of 10x for companies in the U.S. wireless telecommunications space, some of these risks appear to be reflected in Inseego's valuation.

But with headline risk remaining high, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on the company's common shares for now.

Bottom Line

Inseego reported fourth quarter results ahead of muted expectations but issued Q1/2025 well below consensus estimates at that time as the company continues to face headwinds from carrier customers winding down product promotions and managing inventory levels.

On the conference call, management perplexed analysts with ambitious full year sales targets, which would have likely been a stretch even without the recent tariff issues.

For my part, I would expect Inseego to miss expectations by a wide margin this year. While the company's valuation has decreased in recent months, elevated headline risk is causing me to reiterate my "Sell" rating on the company's shares for now.

data.title: Brookfield Preferreds Offer Some High Yield Options
data.description:
All values are in CAD. Prices refer to the TSX-traded stocks and not the OTC ones. This article is written specifically for Canadian investors.

The common stock is a play on the income statement and the bonds are a play on the balance sheet. The preferreds? Well, they stand between the common shares and the debt and almost always, they are also a play on the balance sheet. We will go over some choices for Brookfield Corporation (TSX:BN:CA). As noted above, we are aiming this for Canadian investors and are not interested in doing cross-currency comparisons to preferreds offered in US Dollars.

The Company

It is impossible to not have heard about Brookfield in Canada. The company has turned in a spectacular performance over the last 3 decades that rivals that of some mega-cap names on the US side.

In fact, many call it the Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A) (BRK.B) of Canada. Investors might have seen our work on some of its daughter companies, including Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP), Brookfield Business Partners L.P. (BBU) and Brookfield Property Partners.

Brookfield Asset Management Ltd. (BAM) which was spun off from the mothership recently, is scaling up to be a powerhouse asset manager.

The company is also dipping its toes into the reinsurance and insurance side of things. To top it all off, there is the insider ownership that you would want with such a company.

Why We Are Not Fans Of The Common Shares

Having delved into multiple of its subsidiaries over the past few years, we have seen several issues that make the common shares less than appealing. The first issue is the sheer complexity of the company. Every single entity has an LP structure and a corporate structure. So for example in addition to BEP, you have Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) which is supposed to give you the same kind of returns, but in a corporate structure. The same applies to the other two publicly traded entities. There are also multiple cross-guarantees between organizations. Some of Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP) debt, for example, is guaranteed by Brookfield Corporation. While we are all for doing what it takes to make money, this level of complexity is incredibly hard to get around. Financial Times looked into this labyrinth as well.

While there was no real smoking gun, the maize made it hard to be sure that the common shares were a good value.

We also don't think there is anywhere remotely as much value in the Brookfield real estate as the company claims. Now this may come down to a difference in NAV estimates and cap rates, but we are fairly certain that when the funds from operations (FFO) are negative, you have to pay attention. S&P is following the same plot as us, only they are about a year or two behind. They downgraded Brookfield Property Partners' debt to junk in 2023. That debt was sent further into junk territory last month.

We lowered the issue-level rating on BPY’s unsecured notes to ‘B’ from ‘BB-’ and assigned a ‘6’ recovery rating (rounded estimate: 0%) to the notes.

We affirmed the ‘B+’ issue-level rating on BPR’s senior secured notes and assigned a ‘5’ recovery rating (rounded estimate: 10%).

Source: S&P

Their leverage levels are off the chart.

BPY successfully refinanced a material number of mortgages and repaid some debt to improve its balance sheet modestly in 2024, but its balance sheet remains highly leveraged. As a result, we expect the company to operate with debt-to-EBITDA above the 15x area over the next year. In addition, FCC remains under pressure, ending the year below 1x on our adjusted metrics.

If you look at Brookfield Corporation's NAV estimates, both shown by the company and via analysts, Brookfield Property Partners forms a substantial portion of it. In our view, this is worth a big, fat, zero. Sure, you can have a different opinion, but as a reminder here is their funds from operations (FFO).

Moving On To Debt & Preferreds

While we remain extremely skeptical about Brookfield being worth even half as much as analyst price targets, their debt, and preferreds are a different matter. Simply put, Brookfield has multiple firewalls in place to take sky-high leverage at the subsidiary level and limit damage to the parent. It makes extremely exorbitant fees for the subsidiaries as well and this combination makes the parent appealing. While we disagree with the value of the stock, we actually agree more or less with the credit rating of Brookfield Corporation.

Fitch also notes that the fee-bearing capital is an offset to the very high leverage at the subsidiary level.

Fitch Ratings - New York - 18 Oct 2024: Fitch Ratings has affirmed Brookfield Corporation's (Brookfield) Long-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDR) and those of its debt-issuing subsidiaries at 'A-' and affirmed its Short-Term IDR at 'F2'. The Rating Outlook is Stable.

Key Rating Drivers

Strong Product Line Diversity: The rating affirmations reflect Brookfield's strong competitive position as a global alternative IM, solid investment track record, significant fee-bearing assets under management (FAUM) with strong product line diversity and meaningful exposure to perpetual capital, strong fee-related EBITDA (FEBITDA) margins, distribution-generating capacity from listed affiliates, low leverage on a balance sheet basis, strong funding flexibility and a solid liquidity profile.

Relatively High Cash Flow Leverage: Rating constraints specific to Brookfield include relatively high leverage and low-interest coverage on a FEBITDA basis, above-average exposure to management fees charged on the basis of net asset value (NAV), a large balance sheet, which exposes the capital base to potential valuation declines, and a more complex organizational structure, given the ownership stakes in listed affiliates, Oaktree Capital Management, L.P., Brookfield Wealth Solutions and Brookfield Property Group (BPG).

Source: Fitch

That gets us to our investment options for Canadians.

Brookfield Corporation PFD SHS A SR44 (TSX:BN.PF.H:CA)

There is nothing particularly impressive about the current 5.2% yield on BN.PF.H:CA. But this was issued in late 2020 and has an attractive reset term sheet. It resets on December 31, 2025, at a Government of Canada 5-year (GOC-5) yield plus 4.17%. That spread is incredibly high for the ratings Brookfield has. That was likely the symptom of COVID-19's wrecking ball and investors must remember that this issue was being priced just around when the vaccine was released and credit spreads were still high. Despite the recent turmoil in the markets today, it is a fairly safe bet that this gets called and Brookfield looks to place a lower price security in the future. The yield to call was fairly good so we went for it, buying 2000 shares at $24.00. We also did an alert for our subscribers.

The second alternative is not too bad either. If it is not called your reset is likely to be fairly high. Since this is close to par, your yield would be close to the reset rate. One other thing to note here is that this series has a floor rate of 5%. That is unlikely to be something that people pay attention to, but it is something to keep in mind if ZIRP ever returns. This issue will have better protection than most other resetting preferred shares.

Brookfield Corporation PREF CL A SER 38 (TSX:BN.PF.E:CA)

BN.PF.E:CA was reset just recently.

Brookfield Corporation (“Brookfield”) today announced that it has determined the fixed dividend rate on its Cumulative Class A Preference Shares, Series 38 (the “Series 38 Shares”) (TSX: BN.PF.E) for the five years commencing April 1, 2025, and ending March 31, 2030.

If declared, the fixed quarterly dividends on the Series 38 Shares during the five years commencing April 1, 2025, will be paid at an annual rate of 5.185% ($0.3240625 per share per quarter).

Source: Brookfield.

5.185% sounds ridiculously low, but those were the reset terms, GOC-5 plus 2.55%. Of course, you the investor today, are treated to this price and this chart.

So you get around 7.50% on a stripped basis, locked for 5 years. We think these will appreciate in 2025, and then they can be traded for other issues. But some investors can think of this as a nice locked yield to navigate the market volatility.

Verdict

Both preferred shares are good alternatives for investing in Brookfield. They also offer an eligible dividend which significantly improves their after-tax yield relative to bonds inside taxable accounts. One other way to see their relative value today is to compare them to the fixed rate preferred shares from the same corporation. There are 4 of these, but the one that has the highest current yield is this one. Brookfield Corporation PREF CL A S 36 (TSX:BN.PF.C:CA).

So for the risk of a reset, you are getting a decent premium over these fixed rates. We monitor these and a host of other preferreds that are good ways to generate income without taking excessive risk.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Saga Partners H2 2024 Investor Letter
data.description:
H2 2024 Results

During the second half of 2024, the Saga Portfolio (“the Portfolio”) increased 52.2% net of fees. This compares to the overall increase for the S&P 500 Index, including dividends, of 9.5%.

The cumulative return since inception on January 1, 2017, for the Saga Portfolio is 259.2% net of fees compared to the S&P 500 Index of 204.7%. The annualized return since inception for the Saga Portfolio is 17.3% net of fees compared to the S&P 500’s 14.9%. Please check your individual statement as specific account returns may vary depending on the timing of any contributions throughout the period.

The Questionable Benefits of Frequent Updates and Interpretation of Results

This marks the 20th Saga Portfolio investor letter. When I started writing these letters in 2018, the goal was simple: to explain how I think about investing and to share what we own and why we own it. If someone else were managing my money, that is exactly what I would want to know.

That said, frequent updates can become distractions, shifting focus to short-term stock price movements rather than the fundamental drivers of long-term value. Most other investment strategies’ letters focus on what stock prices did in the past quarter. That is understandable—after all, the goal of investing is to grow wealth, and stock prices are the most immediate and visible way to track that progress. If the last few years have taught us anything, it’s that short-term stock price movements are unpredictable and rarely offer meaningful insight beyond the fact that more people either bought or sold shares during that period. A great business and a well-reasoned investment thesis typically do not change materially from year to year. As David Deutsch defines it, a good explanation is “hard to vary.”

The Saga Portfolio invests with an owner’s mindset because, in my view, that is the only rational way to invest. This naturally leads to low portfolio turnover. The best investments are typically left to compound over many years, provided they remain attractive, with changes only made when more compelling opportunities are discovered.

Given this approach and the fact that past investor letters have covered the Saga Portfolio’s philosophy in detail, I think an annual review of our core holdings is not only sufficient, but preferable. Going forward, I will write one annual investor letter after our core portfolio companies report their full-year fiscal results. However, I plan on sharing shorter, more frequent posts throughout the year on other topics that may be relevant or interesting.

Naturally, with less frequent portfolio updates and a consistent emphasis on focusing on the long term, an important question is: Over what time frame should we judge investment performance?

In my view, one-year portfolio returns are largely random. Over a five-year period, assuming the starting and ending points aren’t unusual periods, we should generally expect to outperform the market. However, if the Portfolio hasn’t outperformed over a 10-year period and there isn’t a good explanation for the underperformance besides “the market is perpetually wrong,” then allocating long-term capital to a simple S&P 500 index fund is likely a better choice.

There’s an interesting paradox in long-term investing: while we ultimately expect our stocks to appreciate over time, true long-term owners and net savers should prefer lower share prices in the short term, all else being equal. It’s similar to homeownership: rising home prices may increase your net worth on paper, but if you plan to buy a second home, lower home prices are in your best interest. Likewise, as long-term investors, we should welcome lower stock prices as opportunities rather than fearing them as setbacks. How one reacts to price declines is a good indicator of whether they are thinking like a business owner or merely speculating—hoping to sell at a higher price to the next buyer.

There’s a saying: understanding the problem is half the solution. Beating the market over the long-term is challenging—especially when managing other people’s money. Few stocks outperform the market over the long- term and building a portfolio of numerous undervalued businesses is inherently difficult. Many of the best opportunities initially look controversial, some will be volatile, and a few will inevitably disappoint. This is why most mutual funds rely on bucketed investing strategies with wide diversification. While this approach makes them more marketable as they appear “less risky,” it has not been a recipe for long-term outperformance, with few exceptions.

In this letter, I explain the challenges of investing in publicly traded stocks through the lens of a concept discussed in the last investor letter—the growth of knowledge. This concept continues to shape how I invest and manage the Saga Portfolio. I’ve included my latest thoughts on our core holdings in the appendix. It has updates on Carvana, Roku, Trupanion, The Trade Desk, our latest investment in Wise PLC, and a postmortem on Redfin’s recently announced sale to Rocket Mortgage. I expect future annual letters will primarily focus on company updates and any portfolio changes, while general investing topics will be shared separately throughout the year.

Business Fragility and the Resilience of Knowledge

History suggests businesses are inherently fragile. Most startups fail, few companies last more than a decade, and even the strongest firms have eventually been replaced. Why is this the case, and what does it mean for investing?

Resilience is the ability of a system to maintain itself despite changes in its environment. It is hard to come by because the laws of physics do not inherently preserve structures beyond their basic elements. In The Science of Can and Can’t, Chiara Marletto describes how resilience emerges in certain systems through catalysts—systems that drive reliable transformations without themselves changing in the process. While planetary orbits and subatomic interactions persist due to the unchanging laws of physics, complex structures—like living organisms, machines, or businesses—require an explanation beyond physical laws to maintain their form. That explanation is knowledge.

Knowledge is a unique kind of catalyst that enables repeatable transformations. Unlike physical catalysts, knowledge is abstract—it can be stored, transferred, and instantiated across different mediums (e.g. DNA, brains, computers, books, etc.). Knowledge is information that is useful in solving a problem. Because it is useful, it tends to perpetuate itself once embedded in a physical system. In this way, knowledge is resilient and can make systems more resilient as well.

For example, the genetic instructions in a specie’s DNA can survive across generations, while an animal's ability to heal from an injury is a form of resilience that inanimate objects like rocks lack. Similarly, businesses embed knowledge into their products, processes, and strategies to enhance longevity—whether through rust-proof coatings on cars, quality control checks, or customer surveys for feedback. Without knowledge, these complex systems would not exist.

There are two known processes for creating knowledge:

  1. Biological evolution, which occurs through genetic variation and natural selection
  2. Human thought, which involves deliberate problem-solving through conjecture and criticism

Natural selection is slower and has limited reach because it is constrained by random mutations. Human-created knowledge, on the other hand, is explanatory, purposeful, and only limited by what is forbidden by the laws of physics. While no machine will ever travel faster than the speed of light, technological progress can continue to redefine what is possible, just as past societies would have viewed air travel or modern medicine as magic.

A corporation functions as an aggregator of resources, a more efficient way of coordinating people and capital to act as a catalyst for solving problems at scale. It facilitates repeatable transformations that create value. Take a car manufacturer: it starts with raw materials like steel, plastic, and rubber, applies a series of specialized processes, and produces a vehicle. What enables this transformation isn’t just the materials, but the embedded knowledge, the instructions, design, and know-how, that guide how those materials are shaped and assembled. It’s this combination of knowledge and resources that create wealth.

But if knowledge is resilient and businesses at their core are built on knowledge, why do most companies fail? The answer lies in two fundamental challenges:

  • Knowledge Can Be Copied

While difficult to create, knowledge is easy to replicate once created. Unlike biological evolution, where genetic adaptations spread only through reproduction, business knowledge can be copied instantly. In a free market, any profitable innovation attracts imitators, eroding excess returns. As a result, most businesses eventually earn just enough above subsistence levels to survive—until an economic shock or competitive shift wipes them out.

Companies can try to prevent their knowledge from being copied by:

  • Legal barriers: Patents, copyrights, or regulatory protections (e.g. AT&T’s former government-sanctioned monopoly)
  • Trade secrets: Proprietary knowledge kept confidential (e.g. Coca Cola’s secret formula)
  • Continuous reinvestment: Using accumulated knowledge to maintain a lead (e.g. Intel’s semiconductor advancements)

Other competitive advantages—like economies of scale, network effects, and switching costs—can also create barriers to entry and make replication difficult.

Consider Microsoft Windows. It remains the dominant PC operating system due to a combination of these competitive advantages. However, it’s worth noting that despite Microsoft’s expertise and dominance in PCs, it failed to capture leadership in smartphone operating systems, where Apple’s iOS and Google’s Android now dominate. This highlights an important point: competitive advantages in one domain do not automatically transfer to another, even with significant resources and motivation.

  • The Creation of New Knowledge

In nature, if the environment changes too rapidly for a species to adapt, it becomes extinct. The same is true in business. Imagine a world where animals were able to create explanatory knowledge and purposefully modify their DNA to evolve. The pace of biological change would accelerate dramatically. Any species failing to adapt would become obsolete.

That hypothetical world is the free market economy, where companies must continuously keep up with competitors’ knowledge to survive. A stable environment favors incumbents, but rapid shifts—driven by new knowledge—can render once dominant firms obsolete. Just as evolutionary adaptations may become ineffective when ecosystems change, businesses can struggle when technological breakthroughs redefine their industries.

Consider newspapers. For decades, the leading paper in a city thrived due to high barriers to entry, including economies of scale and network effects. However, the rise of the Internet transformed the landscape, making their physical production (printing presses) and distribution (delivery trucks) assets obsolete. Newspapers were suddenly forced to compete for readers’ attention on a global scale against digital platforms like Google and Facebook, leaving many struggling to survive.

Businesses only persist by continuously solving problems better than alternative solutions. While competitive advantages may provide temporary protection, history is full of once-dominant companies that declined by clinging to old knowledge.

Take Henry Ford, who revolutionized auto manufacturing with the moving assembly line, making the Model T an enormous success. Ford’s efficiency, achieved through process refinements and vertical integration, gave it a massive cost advantage that other car manufacturers initially struggled to replicate. From the 1910s through the 1920s, Ford was one of the most profitable companies in America. However, Ford’s reluctance to innovate beyond the initial breakthrough allowed General Motors to catch up. GM adopted many of Ford’s techniques but also introduced flexible manufacturing, allowing for greater customization without disrupting production. As consumer preferences shifted toward variety and personalization, GM overtook Ford as the industry leader in the 1930s.

The fragility of businesses is not a flaw of the market, it is a natural consequence of how knowledge works. There is no law of nature that states a company must fail, yet many do because they fail to adapt to a changing environment. A company can have a long history of success and strong cash flows today, but it becomes fragile if it clings to outdated processes, chases short-term profits over long-term value creation, or fails to take promising new technologies seriously.

Competitive advantages provide temporary protection, but true resilience comes from having error correcting mechanisms—systems that allow a company to continuously refine its knowledge, improve its technology, and enhance its value to customers. Ultimately, resilience is not about avoiding problems, it’s about being best equipped to solve them when they inevitably arise.

The Challenge of Investing

While building a resilient business is hard, investing presents a different challenge. The average half-life of a publicly traded stock is ~10 years, meaning half of all companies listing in 2014 had disappeared from the market by 2024, often due to acquisitions or declining performance. Since 1980, over any given 10-year rolling period, around 85% of publicly traded stocks were either delisted or underperformed the S&P 500. Only about15% managed to outperform.

Why do most stocks underperform?

The primary reason many stocks underperform while only a few outperform lies in how the market tends to value businesses. In general, the market anchors a company’s valuation to its recent financial performance.

The job of the market is to evaluate opportunities and allocate resources efficiently. When it correctly assesses a business’s prospects, the stock price reflects its fair value, and is likely to generate average, market-like returns over its remaining life. This efficiency is beneficial for society because it directs capital to where it can be used most productively—to businesses solving problems people want most.

The challenge with assessing a business’s prospects is that the future depends on the creation of new knowledge, which is inherently unpredictable. If we knew the future knowledge that was to be created, then we would already have it available today. Therefore, it is impossible to know exactly what a company will earn in the future. Given this unpredictability, investors often rely on rules of thumb to value a company, the most common one being valuing companies based on recent financial results.

The problem with heuristics is that they are non-explanatory or are based on shallow explanations. Traditional valuation metrics extrapolate from recent results, assuming the past is a reliable predictor of the future. The philosopher Bertrand Russell illustrated the flaw in this kind of inductive reasoning with the story of a chicken: fed each morning, it comes to expect it will always be fed—until one day, it’s slaughtered. The chicken’s theory seemed to be supported right up until the day it didn’t. Similarly, while rules of thumb can be useful in certain contexts, they often rely on weak explanations rather than a deep understanding of a company’s long-term drivers of value.

Relying too heavily on past performance often overlooks the risk posed by future knowledge—new innovations or shifts that can render existing business models obsolete. This is one reason companies are frequently overvalued just before they are disrupted. If their initial valuations failed to anticipate the decline, they are likely to underperform the market. Even when investors recognize a company’s troubles—say, a struggling newspaper—and price the stock at a low multiple of recent earnings, it can still be overvalued if future prospects are worse than anticipated.

On the other hand, resilient companies that continue to innovate and solve increasingly important problems often defy traditional valuation metrics. A stock trading at 30 times earnings might seem expensive by conventional standards, but if the business has a long runway to scale a solution that is hard for others to replicate, it may in fact be undervalued.

Case Studies in Market Mispricing (Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B), Walmart (WMT), Amazon (AMZN))

The charts below show how each company’s stock performed relative to the S&P 500 over any 10-year forward period in its history, with the 10-year forward internal rate of return (IRR) shown on the left axis. This reflects whether the stock outperformed or underperformed the S&P 500 when held for the subsequent decade. On the right axis, a key financial metric is included to provide a comparison of the company’s fundamental performance over time.

What stands out is that many of the best-performing stocks historically outperformed during periods when traditional valuation metrics failed to capture their full long-term potential—and stopped outperforming once current results more accurately reflected future performance, often as reinvestment opportunities diminished and growth slowed.

Berkshire Hathaway’s historical performance illustrates this concept well. Book value per share, compounded at ~20% over six decades. However, most of its outperformance occurred from the 1960s to the 1980s, with returns aligning more closely to the S&P 500 after the year 2000, when its growth slowed.

Walmart became one of the most successful retailers in history by developing a superior discount retailing model built on operational efficiency and supply chain innovation. Yet in its earlier years, the market remained skeptical of its low-margin, high-volume approach. Throughout the 1970s and 1980s, Walmart’s stock was consistently undervalued as traditional metrics failed to capture the scale and durability of its advantage. It wasn’t until the late 1990s, when reinvestment opportunities waned and growth slowed—that valuation metrics began to align more closely with its intrinsic value. From then on, the stock largely tracked the broader market.

Amazon, the more recent retail (and now data center) giant, presents another compelling example. It discovered an improved retail concept with e-commerce and then scaled that service across the country. Traditional valuation metrics based on recent financial performance consistently underestimated its potential. As a result, Amazon had one of the highest returning stocks over the last 30 years.

It is also worth noting that despite their exceptional long-term returns, these stocks often endured significant volatility and steep drawdowns. Amazon, for instance, experienced regular declines of more than 50%, including a staggering 95% drop during its early years.

Exceptional long-term returns are rarely smooth; they are often littered with periods of intense market skepticism and steep declines. However, in many cases, these companies were still undervalued even before any major price drawdowns. Investors who were able to hold onto their shares throughout the volatility were rewarded with attractive long-term returns. Trying to trade in and out of a stock of a company that one thinks is significantly undervalued based on guessing short-term stock price movements is more likely to hurt rather than help results.

Sam Walton expressed a similar sentiment in his autobiography Made in America, “I believe the folks who have done the best with Wal-Mart stock are those who have studied the company, who have understood our strengths and our management approach, and who, like me, have just decided to invest with us for the long run.”

Conclusion

Many investors seek a universal magic formula or pattern to identify undervalued stocks. However, history shows that no single heuristic—whether based on valuation multiples, margins, capital intensity, or growth rates— reliably predicts long-term outperformance. The top-performing stocks of one decade had no greater probability of outperforming in the next.

That’s because rational investing isn’t about blindly applying heuristics or extrapolating past data. It’s not about deterministic cause-and-effect relationships. It’s about developing a good explanatory framework—one that begins with understanding the problems people are trying to solve, the range of solutions available, and how a company fits into that landscape. It involves studying how a business creates value, its resilience, competitive advantages, scalability, and why the market might be misjudging it. While predictions may be a byproduct of deep understanding, without a good explanatory framework, expectations about the future are meaningless.

More often than not, this process results in no actionable investment ideas because a compelling explanation fails to emerge. The investing process is about generating potential ideas and then trying to disprove them. The few ideas that can survive that scrutiny are the ones worth investing in. Even when a strong case for undervaluation exists, success is never guaranteed. Better explanations guide better decision-making, but certainty about the future is never attainable because it will always be inherently unpredictable.

This approach leads to a simple strategy: invest in the most compelling opportunities as if we had to own them for their remaining life. It shifts the focus from guessing short-term stock price movements to understanding the core drivers of long-term value creation. As a result, the Saga Portfolio often owns companies that might appear unconventional or even controversial at first glance, and may experience greater volatility (2022, anyone?). But over time, I expect these businesses, as a group, to compound value faster than the broader market.

As always, it is a privilege to manage your capital. The success of the Saga Portfolio depends on an investor base that is aligned and committed to the long-term—a mindset that is essential for navigating the market’s inevitable ups and downs.

Please don’t hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or comments.

Sincerely,

data.title: Tesla: Even More Bad News
data.description:
Tesla, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:TSLA) shareholders, with CEO Elon Musk at the forefront, had quite bad months. Since mid-December 2024, the stock price has crashed by over 45%, leaving many investors tempted to increase or initiate positions in this business. Does it surprise me? Not at all, as Tesla has gathered a lot of recognition over the years and many perceive it as a cutting-edge enterprise with long-term tailwinds, plus investors often perceive heavily "sold-out" businesses as cheap by default.

However, it's not that simple, and I'm afraid I have some bad news for TSLA's shareholders. I won't sugar-coat it, so let's make my stance clear from the get-go. Tesla remains a "strong sell" for me and I think there's still plenty of room for the stock price to fall even further. Within this article, I'll build upon the rationale I presented in my last coverage based on the latest market data and geopolitical issues.

Tesla keeps losing its market share; rapid sales declines

Declining sales are naturally a concern, but circumstances also matter. Is the market size shrinking or expanding? It's important, as declining sales within a shrinking cake is much different from losing sales within an expanding one. Unfortunately, it's the latter case for Tesla. Let's start with the U.S.

The EV market in the U.S. keeps on growing and recorded ~11% sales growth during Q1 2025 vs. Q1 2024. At the same time, Tesla lost around 9% of its sales, which definitely raises concerns. While TSLA remains the major player with ~44% market share in the U.S., it used to have a share of ~51%. With declining sales within a growing market, Tesla loses its market share on behalf of other players, often more traditional vehicle manufacturers. For instance:

Car buyers are moving away from Teslas and toward models like General Motors’ Chevrolet Equinox electric vehicle, which starts at around $35,000 and can travel more than 300 miles on a charge, Cox Automotive, the research firm, said in a report.

Moving onto Europe, regardless of whether you look at France or Germany, or Scandinavian countries like Sweden, Denmark, or Norway, Tesla is suffering. In most European markets, Tesla recorded a sales drop for a third consecutive month:

Tesla last month registered 3,157 car sales in France, 911 in Sweden, and 2,211 in Norway, dropping 36.83%, 63.9%, and 1% respectively from last year, official data showed.

A similar tendency occurred in Germany where Tesla sold over 2200 cars in March 2025 (down by over 40% vs. March 2024). The shining point of Tesla's presence in Europe? The UK, Tesla's largest European market, where the Company sold over 7200 cars in March 2025. On a quarterly basis, TSLA's sales in the UK rose by a low-single digit percentage, but the problem of losing market share remains:

In the UK, Tesla's biggest market in Europe, its quarterly sales rose 3.5% to 12,628, but its share of the EV market dropped by over 4 percentage points to 10.7% as the country reported a record of over 68,000 EV sales in the quarter, data by research group New AutoMotive showed.

The U.S. vs. China trade war increases the risk around Tesla

Even Elon Musk himself admitted that the impact of an escalating trade war could be significant for Tesla:

How so? Well, although it has production facilities in the U.S., it's important to note that manufacturers need numerous parts to produce a vehicle - many of which are imported from Mexico, Canada, China, or Europe. Therefore, TSLA's production costs are likely to increase, and given already losing market share - do you think Tesla will be able to fully transfer them onto its customers? I don't.

On top of that, the escalation between the U.S. and China already has some effect on TSLA's business moves, as it no longer takes orders for models X and S in China:

Tesla has suspended taking new orders for Model S and Model X vehicles, according to checks by Reuters and The New York Times. Notably, new orders for the two imported models were also no longer listed on the automaker's WeChat mini program account at the time of publication.

Both the Model S and Model X are made in the United States and imported to China. While the Austin-based company did not give a reason for the move, the dueling tariff war between China and the U.S. has altered the playing field for EV sellers in both nations.

Sold-off does NOT mean cheap - It's to the contrary for Tesla

Let's talk about some numbers for context. I'm an M&A advisor, which means I participate in transaction processes regarding buying and selling businesses. Have I sold businesses for single-digit multiples? Yes. High teens? Yes. I had some transactions closed at 20-30x EV/EBITDA range, but these were related to top-tier businesses I had the pleasure to cooperate with.

The stock market works similarly - the better the business the higher the multiple. Multiple ranges also differ industry-to-industry based on growth expectations - that's how technological players uphold relatively high multiples.

Where does Tesla stand in all of this? It has a forward-looking EV-to-EBITDA of ~46x and over 60x on a trailing-twelve months basis. Normally, the disparity between forward-looking and realized multiples hints at upcoming EBITDA growth through either increased business scale or profitability. However, in the case of Tesla - I find it hard to believe its forward-looking results will improve so substantially (if at all) to cover the disparity between 46x and 60x multiples.

There's even more to it. Why would I buy Tesla's 46x forward-looking EV/EBITDA with the likes of Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) at ~11.8x, and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) at ~20x? I consider both much more successful businesses that 1. keep increasing their sales, 2. face less significant geopolitical risks, and 3. don't come with the brand damage related to Elon's engagement in politics (given that most EV buyers are left-leaning).

With all that in mind, Tesla remains a 'strong sell' for me. It doesn't deserve such a high valuation and even after the substantial stock price fall, it's far from cheap and the crash - is far from over. Cheers!

data.title: Microsoft: A Defensive Fortress In The Tariff Storm
data.description:
Market jitters are at an all-time high as the global economy braces for ongoing uncertainties in trade policy, particularly those related to the fickle state of U.S. reciprocal tariffs. The adverse implications of said policy shift, spanning increased import costs, global supply chain disruptions, and an economic downturn continue to loom large. And ensuing investor angst has accordingly prompted a reassessment of which stocks could demonstrate the most resilience amidst the wreckage.

Following months of moderating valuations in U.S. equities, made worse by tariff uncertainties in recent weeks, we believe Microsoft (MSFT) has emerged as a compelling long-term investment opportunity at current levels. The company’s software-centric, yet diverse business model also presents as a competitive advantage against its megacap tech peers, as it provides Microsoft with incremental resilience against impending tariff-driven vulnerabilities.

Inherently Low Direct Exposure to Tariffs

One of the most compelling strengths exhibited by Microsoft amidst the ongoing tariff standoff between the U.S. and the rest of the world is its minimal reliance on hardware. Given the reciprocal tariffs have been primarily levied on the import of goods, Microsoft’s software-centric business model provides it with significant mitigation against exposure to the ensuing cost and demand implications.

Specifically, Microsoft currently generates more than 90% of its revenue from software and services sales through its productivity and business processes (“PBP”) and intelligent cloud (“IC”) segments. Meanwhile, its slim hardware revenue mix generated from Surface and Xbox console sales, which are most prone to tariff cost implications, have already been on a consistent secular decline in recent years.

This represents a favourable sales mix for Microsoft amidst the tariff-heavy environment. Recall that the reciprocal tariffs announced to date have reached as high as 145% for China, with the situation still evolving. This is bound to squeeze margins for companies with significant reliance on global supply chains – a risk that Microsoft’s business model is inherently protected against.

Recession-Resistant Product Roadmap

Admittedly, no company can be entirely immune to impending macroeconomic uncertainties stemming from the aggressive tariff policy shift. Not only do tariffs raise the cost of goods, but the ensuing tightening of consumer spending could also trigger recessionary risks and unleash broader demand risks.

Specifically, economists are currently predicting a slower pace of 2025 U.S. GDP growth due to ongoing government spending cuts and tariff uncertainties, which risk triggering a recession. The average U.S. GDP growth estimate has decreased from +2.2% y/y earlier in the year to now +1.7% y/y. This represents a whopping 25% drop in the forward growth curve, which highlights the impending headwind facing corporate earnings. Although Microsoft operates under a software-centric business model, its business is bound to be indirectly exposed to tariff-driven cyclical headwinds, nonetheless.

Yet unlike its services-oriented megacap tech peers like Google (GOOG / GOOGL) and Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft’s differentiated by its sprawling portfolio of mission-critical offerings to both consumer and enterprise end-markets in a cyclical downturn. Despite its massive installed base, Microsoft continues to deliver strong double-digit percentage growth, thanks to persistent monetization improvements underpinned by its provision of essential solutions.

This is evident in the company’s recent disclosure of better-than-expected growth across both its Microsoft 365 Consumer and Commercial offerings. Specifically, Microsoft 365 Commercial Cloud revenue was a core driver of PBP segment growth during F2Q. The product generated revenue growth of 16% y/y, driven by strong uptake of E5 and Copilot add-ons. The company’s improving monetization of its commercial installed base was also evident in the fact that subscription revenue growth of 16% y/y outpaced seat growth of 7% y/y by almost 2x over the same period. This suggests resonation from enterprise end-markets with the value proposition of incremental productivity gains offered through new M365 offerings like Copilot and E5.

We expect these resilient monetization trends to persist ahead of the looming economic downturn, as optimization efforts resurface. This is already evident at tech companies like Shopify (SHOP) and Meta, which are increasingly touting ways to alleviate margins with the adoption of AI tools over incremental manual labour. Although the impending tariffs risk further curtailment in the hiring market, resilient monetization of Microsoft’s existing installed base via value-adding add-ons could offset moderation in seat growth.

Meanwhile, Microsoft 365 Consumer also delivered better-than-expected growth at 8% y/y during F2Q, despite an unfavourable mix shift to the lower-priced Basic tier. This suggests inelastic demand for Microsoft’s cloud-based productivity software in consumer end-markets, and limited impact to the product’s unit economics even amidst a downshift in consumer preference for lower-priced offerings. This also reinforces expectations for limited churn, despite the 30% price hike implemented in January for Copilot-equipped subscriptions (Basic tier pricing remains unchanged at $100/year).

In addition to Microsoft 365, Microsoft’s increasing cloud market share through Azure is also expected to mitigate its exposure to the impending tariff implications. Because of Azure’s comprehensive tech stack in supporting the ongoing AI transformation, it’s become deeply embedded in enterprise workflows. This is evident in ballooning demand for Azure compute capacity, increasing Microsoft Fabric and Power BI MAUs, and accelerating Azure AI Foundry run times disclosed by management during the F2Q earnings update. Businesses, regardless of economic conditions, are unlikely to cut back on these essentials, especially as compute capacity, data, and application development and deployment tools have become indispensable in the AI-first era. This accordingly generates sticky demand for Microsoft’s Azure revenue stream, which transcends economic cycles.

The anticipated resilience in Microsoft’s Azure revenues – especially those generated from AI services – is also corroborated by incremental compute capacity that’s coming online later this year. Recall that compute demand has been consistently outpacing supply at Microsoft since the onset of the AI sensation. But the consistently elevated level of capex deployed towards data center and server capacity over the past several quarters are expected to start coming online in the second half of FY25. This should improve monetization of pent-up demand in the coming quarters, which is supported by the significant acceleration in commercial bookings growth.

Admittedly, discretionary spending in both consumer and enterprise end-markets are poised to falter amidst renewed concerns that the elevated tariffs will reignite an inflationary environment. However, Microsoft’s diverse portfolio of mission-critical offerings, which are integral to enterprise operations, are expected to stay resistant against the emerging growth risks. This resilience in Microsoft’s core software and cloud computing offerings is especially significant amid the tariff disruptions, as it promises stability in uncertain times.

Unmatched Pricing Power

The mission-critical role of Microsoft’s product portfolio also yields unmatched pricing power even in the face of broader macroeconomic uncertainties. Businesses that have invested in Microsoft’s ecosystem – whether it’s the productivity software suite or Azure – are less likely to upend and leave given migration inefficiencies and ensuing egress charges.

This accordingly allows Microsoft to partially or even completely pass on any tariff-related costs to end customers in the form of price hikes. And the effectiveness of which is evident in Microsoft’s consistent pace of double-digit growth, despite its massive installed base and periodic price hikes across Microsoft 365, Office and Azure offerings over the years.

While its tech peers might find challenge in preserving margins due to their inherent reliance on global supply chains and subsequent exposure to tariffs, Microsoft’s software-centric business model and pricing power represents an invaluable advantage. This is further corroborated by Microsoft’s expectations for slight expansion in its FY25 operating margins, despite consistent acceleration in AI investments over the past year. The guidance implies stellar scalability, which will provide an incremental buffer against the increasing costs of growth that stem indirectly from tariffs.

Fundamental Considerations

As discussed in the earlier section, economists currently anticipate a 25% drop in NTM U.S. GDP growth estimates. We’ve considered this as a proxy for gauging the potential tariff-induced impacts to Microsoft’s earnings.

Our downside scenario forecast predicts FY26 revenue to grow at 10% y/y, which represents an approximate 25% discount to the 14% y/y growth anticipated in our previously discussed base case forecast. Despite ongoing market uncertainties, we don’t expect such a materially sustained drop in Microsoft’s forward growth curve. However, we believe the conservative assumption would be an adequate gauge for the worst-case scenario. This will be key to determining the price at which Microsoft has truly become an attractive opportunity. The lowered baseline revenue is expected to alter our prediction for Microsoft’s five-year CAGR to 9% in the downside scenario, versus 12% in the base case scenario.

Meanwhile, adjustments to Microsoft’s profit margins are immaterial in the downside scenario forecast, as the impending tariffs are unlikely to significantly disrupt the cost structure of its software-centric business model. This is further corroborated by Microsoft’s pricing power for its products, which will further help the company with preserving margins.

Valuation Considerations

Based on downside scenario fundamental projections, we expect Microsoft to present as a buying opportunity at any price level below $380 per share.

The downside scenario price target is computed under the discounted cash flow method. The cash flow projections considered are taken in conjunction with the downside scenario fundamental forecast discussed in the earlier section.

A 9.5% WACC in line with Microsoft’s risk profile and capital structure is applied to the analysis. The WACC calculation includes slight adjustments compared to the previously discussed base case assumptions to reflect changes in broader market and macroeconomic factors since (e.g., risk free rate, equity risk premium).

We’ve also applied a 3.5% perpetual growth rate on FY30E EBITDA in determining Microsoft’s terminal value. The 3.5% perpetual growth rate applied is in line with the higher range of long-term economic growth across Microsoft’s core operating regions. We believe the assumption is reflective of the pace in which Microsoft can grow sustainably into perpetuity at which it reaches steady-state in the long-run.

Conclusion

Although not entirely immune to impending macroeconomic risks, Microsoft’s relative stability amid ongoing tariff uncertainties makes the stock uniquely positioned against broader market volatility. In addition to its software-centric business model and resilient portfolio of mission-critical offerings, we believe Microsoft management is also taking a proactive stance in mitigating the company’s exposure to tariff headwinds.

Specifically, management’s repeatedly reiterated their promise to actively scale and adjust the allocation of capex spend based on “both near-term and long-term demand signals”. This was evident in Microsoft’s recent cancellation of certain data center leases, despite reiterating its $80 billion FY25 capex outlook. The undertaking suggests an earlier-than-expected shift from long-lived assets to “short-lived assets which are more correlated to revenue growth”. Coupled with incremental cloud computing capacity coming online in the coming quarters, which underpins Azure reacceleration, we believe Microsoft’s growth outlook remains intact and resistant against looming macroeconomic uncertainties.

As tariffs threaten to reshape global trade and upend forward economic growth prospects, Microsoft’s ability to adapt, innovate and deliver essential services to both consumer and enterprise end-markets make it a compelling defensive play. With the stock now also trading near our downside scenario price target, we believe Microsoft has presented itself as an opportunity amidst the broader market wreckage.

data.title: Seagate Technology Q3 Preview: Tariff Uncertainty Keeps Me Hesitant For Now
data.description:
Introduction

Seagate Technology Holdings plc (NASDAQ:STX) is due to report earnings on the 22nd of April, so I wanted to go through some numbers as well as what I am looking out for from the conference call in terms of updated comments on the company’s outlook, now that tariffs are the talk of the town.

What To Expect

Analysts expect the company to make $1.75 of adjusted EPS on $2.14B in sales. Sequentially, the top line declined by 8%, and a decent 29% growth y/y. How do these numbers stack up against what the management guided? For the upcoming quarter, the management expects the company will do around $2.1B in sales, plus or minus $150m, which is in line with the consensus midpoint value. Adjusted EPS to be in the range of $1.70, plus or minus $0.20, which is slightly below consensus estimates at the midpoint. Now, how likely is the company will beat or at least meet these estimates? As you can see below, the company did better at beating EPS estimates and much worse at sales.

It is also worth noting that there have been 12 EPS revisions to the downside as well as 4 revisions to the upside in the last 90 days. So, it is hard to tell what is going to happen.

What I’m Looking For

In terms of the company’s top-line growth, we can see that the demand for its products is alive and well. A 29% growth supports this view, so what is going to help it maintain such growth going forward? HDD revenues are the most promising growth for the company, with an impressive projected growth of 45% for 2025, driven by rising demand from major cloud providers due to continual AI advancements.

Cloud providers are driving HDD demand. All these enterprises require mass-capacity storage due to AI. The company’s Lyve Cloud platform is positioned very well to benefit from this trend going forward. What I’ll be looking out for is what these enterprises are going to do in terms of capex in AI spending. Just a couple of quarters ago, the big tech said they were going to spend a lot more this year on AI capex than they did in 2024, however, that was before all the tariff uncertainty. Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) will be reporting at the end of this month, and who knows if there will be any updates in the capex outlook due to tariff uncertainty. It would hurt many companies like STX if they were looking to reduce spending in this environment.

MSFT already paused its 2 gigawatts of US/ EU data-center projects due to oversupply and tariff uncertainty. Investors are already showing skepticism towards these companies as concerns about the returns from AI spending are worth it in the end, especially now that DeepSeek, from Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (DEEPSEEK), came in with a much lower cost model. So, it will be interesting to hear what the management has to say about the strength in the demand from these companies going forward.

In terms of the operational outlook for 2025, the company recently attended the Morgan Stanley Conference, in which CFO Gianluca Romano said that Seagate’s Heat-Assisted Magnetic Recording or HAMR technology, which enables 36TB drives, is ramping up with a major cloud provider, and is expected to go into full-scale production in late 2025. This is meant to boost exabyte output on the same unit capacity. Additionally, the company expects further operational efficiency by shifting to MR, or Magnetoresistive, technology, which will improve sensitivity and efficiency in HDDs, enabling higher capacities and a much better performance. This is also projected to boost the company’s sequential revenue growth this year.

Now, all this is great to hear. However, what is going to happen in reality may be quite different if the Trump Administration is going to go ahead and implement some sort of tariffs in the future. Over the weekend, President Trump put exemptions on a lot of consumer electronics including HDDs from the 125% tariffs on Chinese imports. This will likely help the company tame its costs for now. However, given the flip-flop nature of the “art of the deal,” I am not fully certain that this is the end of tariff wars right now, and I wouldn’t be surprised if Trump goes ahead and scares the markets once again with another round of sweeping tariffs. The original tariffs on Chinese imports are still in effect, so I expect to hear something regarding increased costs in the upcoming earnings call.

Closing Comments

Seagate Technology Holdings plc does have a decent growth runway going forward, and I expect it to perform well in the future. However, because of this uncertainty regarding the ripple effects of tariffs still to come and the ones that are already in effect, I am hesitant to give it a buy rating for now.

I am going to tune in to the company’s conference call as well as the big tech earnings calls to hear what they have to say regarding their capex spending in 2025 and whether the idea has changed since tariffs became the prominent talking point around the world.

Seagate’s share price has dropped considerably since January and is becoming a lot more attractive. Thus, I will revisit the company in a few months after we hopefully get a lot more certainty regarding the economic outlook and how these tariffs going to affect companies like Seagate.

data.title: Comcast: Something Epic Is Coming
data.description:
I don't know about you, but I have always loved theme parks. Some of my greatest memories have been the couple of times that I have visited theme parks owned by The Walt Disney Company (DIS), with examples being the Magic Kingdom, Disney’s Animal Kingdom, Disney’s Hollywood Studios, and Epcot, with the last of these being my personal favorite. However, next month, on May 22nd, something "epic" is coming onto the scene that risks Disney's position as the go-to destination in Florida. That is none other than Epic Universe, which is a new theme park being opened at Universal Orlando Resort, which is owned by NBCUniversal, a subsidiary of entertainment giant Comcast Corporation (NASDAQ:CMCSA).

This multi-year initiative will be the first theme park opened in Florida in 26 years. We don't know the exact cost of it, but third party estimates have put a price tag on it of $7.7 billion. That covers 750 acres of space. To put that in perspective, even Disney’s Animal Kingdom, which is the largest of the parks at Walt Disney World, occupies only 580 acres. Even if you added up Epcot, Magic Kingdom, and Disney’s Hollywood Studios together, you would get a size of only 547 acres.

This is a big gamble being made by Comcast. But all the data currently available suggests to me that it's likely to be a big winner. We won't know for at least a year or two exactly how successful this theme park will be. Furthermore, there is uncertainty because of broader economic concerns that could harm the U.S. economy in the near term. But when you add on top of this how cheap shares of Comcast currently are, I would argue that the company makes for a clear "strong buy" candidate at this point in time.

Years in the making

Epic Universe is an initiative that has been years in the making. Originally, construction on it began in August of 2019. But because of the COVID-19 pandemic, work was halted in July of 2020 and wasn't resumed until March of 2021. With COVID-19 now a distant memory, and the economy having recovered significantly, the theme park is getting close to launch. In fact, the management team at Comcast has said that opening day will be May 22nd of this year. That's a little more than a month from the present moment.

Inclusive of this major investment by the company, Comcast has invested over $10 billion in Universal destinations projects from 2018 through 2024. This is if we include its investments in California, Texas, and Nevada as well. Management describes Epic Universe as a technologically advanced theme park that will operate as a,

"weeklong, four-theme-park vacation destination featuring three theme parks, one water park, CityWalk dining retail, and entertainment district, and 11 hotels."

Most of the hotels, to be fair, were built outside of this initiative. Only three of them are new and related to Epic Universe.

The first of its themed worlds is called The Wizarding World of Harry Potter – Ministry of Magic. Although JK Rowling, the author of the series, has become a very controversial figure in recent years, the magic is still alive with the Harry Potter brand. This is evidenced not only by this theme park, but also by spin-off films in recent years and the fact that Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) will begin filming an entire Harry Potter television series this summer.

The second world included in Epic Universe is appropriately called Super Nintendo World and will feature different attractions like being able to challenge Bowser on the park’s Mario Kart ride, venturing into Donkey Kong Country, and more. Another world called How to Train Your Dragon – Isle of Berk will focus on the DreamWorks Animations hit movies and will bring with it a Viking themed village that includes Dragons and other attractions. Epic Universe will also include Dark Universe, a theme park dedicated to horror movie monsters such as Frankenstein. On top of all of this, the company will also have a ride, entertainment, dining, and shopping district by the name of Celestial Park.

Management expects Epic Universe to have a major positive impact, not only on shareholders, but on the local economy. For instance, the company claims already that it's direct investment into the theme park has had an $11 billion economic impact across the U.S. In the first full year of operation, the park is expected to generate around $2 billion for the state of Florida. After its first year of operations, it anticipates generating $386 million in state and local taxes, $120 million in property taxes, and $500 million in sales taxes for the state. They expect a $13 million tax inflow associated with tourist development taxes, and the firm is investing $230 million into improving public facilities as part of this. Ultimately, the company expects to create 17,500 new jobs nationwide in the first full year that this project is in effect. Around 10,290 of those jobs are likely to occur within Epic Universe itself, with the remainder being off site. This is all on top of the 65,000 temporary jobs created as a result of the park’s construction.

We have no idea how much revenue will be generated from this initiative. However, the sales tax estimates provided by management give us a clue. A good estimate for the blended sales tax rate in Florida is 6.5%. This would imply annual revenue, starting after the first year of operations, of $7.69 billion. To put this in perspective, in 2024, Comcast’s theme parks alone ended up generating $8.62 billion in sales. That was up from the $7.54 billion reported two years earlier.

The company has come to understand over the years that theme parks are a major source of not only revenue, but also cash flow. Take Disney as an example. If we look at just the Experiences operations of the entertainment behemoth, revenue from 2022 to 2024 rose from $28.09 billion to $34.15 billion. Over this window of time, theme park admissions revenue on its own grew from $8.60 billion to $11.17 billion. But of course, the revenue includes other things as well, such as resorts and vacations, licensing out content, and more.

Even though Experiences accounted for 36.7% of the revenue that Disney generated in 2024, the operations in it were responsible for 59.4% of the firm's total segment profits. Unfortunately, there are significant comparability issues when it comes to how Comcast reports its theme park data and how Disney reports its data. Comcast is much more obscure with its results. However, to the best that I can, I was able to show below the segment EBITDA and EBITDA margins generated by Disney’s Experiences business and how those figures stand up against what Comcast achieves.

These numbers are within a fairly narrow range of one another. So already, Comcast understands how profitable the theme park business can be. Doubling down on it, literally, makes a great deal of sense. At the end of the day, Epic Universe is certainly the largest initiative being worked on by Comcast at this time. But it's not the only destination concept it is putting its efforts toward. In Frisco, Texas, the company is building the first-ever Universal Kids Resort, which is a family theme park concept that will include an integrated 300 room hotel. This would include featured lands themed around DreamWorks Animations characters such as Shrek, Puss in Boots, Trolls, and Gabby’s Dollhouse. It will also include Nickelodeon’s SpongeBob SquarePants, Illumination’s Minions, and even Jurassic World. This is currently expected to open in 2026. In Hollywood, the company is working on a new rollercoaster dedicated to the Fast & Furious franchise, plus it opened a DreamWorks Land in Orlando in 2024.

It's truly unclear at this time what will happen consider the economy. It wouldn't be shocking to see traction struggle after an initial spike in traffic. However, in looking at ticket pricing, I regularly ran into the message that volumes on the company's website were too high. An image of that experience can be seen above. What I do know is that, over the long haul, Florida has been a major destination for tourists. In 2022, the state saw 137.4 million tourists come in. By 2024, that number had grown to 142.9 million.

The vast majority of these are domestic tourists. But the company does receive over 10 million international tourists each year. We don't have data for 2024, but we do know that the out of state spending in 2023 associated with tourism for the state was $131 billion. This represents an increase over the $124.9 billion generated just one year earlier.

The state saw $8.5 billion in tax revenue associated with the tourism industry, with 84% of that being sales tax. Local governments saw another $8.6 billion in tax revenue from this activity. Not surprisingly, tourism is the second-largest source of employment in Florida with 1.39 million workers. This is only behind the health and social services category at 1.57 million. In 2023, the industry was responsible for supporting $76 billion in wages. That's an increase over the $73 billion seen one year earlier and represented the largest wage support in the state's history for that space.

Even if Epic Universe doesn't pan out in the way that investors hope, there is no denying that Comcast is an attractive prospect. From 2023 to 2024, the company saw adjusted net income grow from $16.49 billion to $16.94 billion. Adjusted operating cash flow, which strips out changes in working capital, expanded from $27.49 billion to $32.62 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business expanded from $37.63 billion to $38.07 billion. In the chart below, you can see how cheap shares are on an absolute basis. These are incredibly low multiples as I see it.

Takeaway

At the end of the day, I believe that Epic Universe makes for an interesting opportunity for Comcast. Even as things stand with the company at the moment, shares are attractively priced. Recent financial performance is encouraging and, while we could see weakness in the near term, I see no reason why the long-term outlook wouldn't be positive given the tourism numbers that we have seen for Florida. Add all of this together, and I do believe that Comcast makes for a very solid "strong buy" candidate. And I'm not the only one. Management even recently announced a new $15 billion share buyback program. Given how cheap the stock is, this seems to be a no-brainer to me.

data.title: Dreamland Limited Seeks U.S. IPO On High Valuation And Risks
data.description:
Dreamland Is Growing But Valuation And Risks Are High

Dreamland Limited (TDIC) has filed to raise $9 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to an SEC F-1 registration statement.

TDIC provides event management services in the greater Hong Kong region.

The company is growing revenue from a tiny base, but is thinly capitalized and is seeking wildly excessive valuation multiples at IPO.

My outlook on the TDIC IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Dreamland Do?

Hong Kong, PRC-based Dreamland Limited was founded to assist event organizers in their themed touring walk-through experience events in Hong Kong.

Management is headed by founder, and CEO Ms. Seto Wai Yue, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2016 and was previously the founder of The Stairs, a luxury retail store and has experience in the development and management of a well-known cartoon character.

The company’s primary offerings include the following:

  • Event Organizing.

  • Planning.

  • Promoting.

  • Managing.

  • Merchandise Design.

  • Store Management.

As of September 30, 2024, Dreamland has booked a fair market value investment of $261,300 from investors, including the founder.

The company has more than eight years of operation and has completed over 30 large-scale events and provided related services to IP owners and event organizers of various sizes.

The firm counts more than 30 brand owners, 15 IP owners and event organizers as clients with their bases of operations in Hong Kong, Taiwan, Japan, and the US.

Regarding related party transactions, the company and its founder have extensive related party relationships and transaction history, as detailed here.

Market Overview For Dreamland

According to a 2025 market research report by Grand View Research, the Chinese MICE (Meetings, Incentives, Conferences, and Events) market was estimated at $81.2 billion in 2024 and is expected to exceed $139 billion by 2030.

This represents an expected CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) of a fairly robust 9.5% from 2025 to 2030.

The primary reasons for this expected growth are expected to be the Incentives segment, which is forecasted to produce the highest growth through 2030, although the Meetings segment was the largest revenue-generating segment in 2024.

Various competitive or other industry participants include the following:

  • BCD.

  • Conference Care.

  • ATPI Limited.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and expected future growth trajectory of the Chinese MICE market from 2018 through 2030:

  • Full suite of offerings, creative capabilities, and network of partnerships.

  • Concentrated revenue, fixed price project risks, limited market penetration.

  • Digital growth plans, geographic expansion, moving up the value chain.

Threats

  • Intense and fragmented competition, regulatory unpredictability, IP licensing risks.

Dreamland’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Growing topline revenue from a small base.

IPO Details For Dreamland

Dreamland intends to raise $9 million in gross proceeds from an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, with the company offering 1.34 million shares at a proposed midpoint price of $4.50 per share.

The sole existing shareholder, the founder, has indicated that she intends to sell 660,000 shares in the IPO. This is a negative signal and is uncommon for the founder and CEO to do.

Even so, the founder will have a significant majority of voting control immediately after the IPO, also resulting in key-person risk and potential related transaction risks.

No outside investors have shown a desire to purchase block shares in the IPO.

With a completed IPO, the firm’s enterprise value would be approximately $135 million, not including the effects of underwriter over-allotment options.

The float to outstanding shares ratio (ex-underwriter over-allotments) will be approximately 6.45%, so the stock will be a ‘low float’ stock subject to potentially much higher volatility in open market trading.

Management said the dilution in net tangible book value per share for investors in the IPO is expected to be $4.36, which is within the typical range of companies at IPO.

The company, directors, executive officers, and 5% or greater shareholders will be subject to the standard 180-day shareholder sale lock-up period.

Management disclosed it intends to apply the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

approximately 10% to acquire a multi-territorial IP license;

approximately 10% to set up our own ticketing platform;

approximately 15% for possible strategic acquisitions;

approximately 15% to expand our marketing department and financing and administration department;

approximately 10% for upgrading our enterprise resource planning system;

approximately 10% for repayment of loans made by Ms. Seto to us in connection with the payment of costs and expenses in connection with this offering and obtaining a listing of our Shares on the Nasdaq. The facility is unsecured, and interest-free in the aggregate amount of up to HK$12,000,000. As of the date of this prospectus, the amount drawn down is approximately HK$3.7 million and is repayable on the earlier of the completion of this offering and September 30, 2025; and

the balance for working capital and other corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC).

The company's online roadshow is not available.

Regarding outstanding legal proceedings, the leadership said the firm is not subject to any legal claims or arbitration that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or results from operations.

The only listed bookrunner of the IPO is Bancroft Capital, LLC.
